title: Chris Claremont

'''Chris Claremont''' (born [[November 30]], [[1950s#1950|1950]] in [[Wikipedia:London|London]], England) is a comic book writer, who worked on a few issues in [[Star Wars (1977)|Marvel's ''Star Wars'']] series, including the multipart ''[[World of Fire!]]'' for Marvel UK (reprinted in ''[[Star Wars Omnibus: Wild Space Volume 1]]''), [[Star Wars Annual (1977) 1|''Star Wars Annual'' (1977) 1]], and others. His contributions include co-creating the characters [[Kharys]] and [[Aron Peacebringer]].

==Biography==
===Writer profile===
Claremont is regarded as one of the hallmark [[Marvel Comics]] writers, having written one of the flagship titles, the [[Wikipedia:Uncanny X-Men|''Uncanny X-Men'']], for sixteen consecutive years, bringing the title from obscurity to one of the most successful comics of all time. Many of his stories contained the social undercurrent of mutants being a harassed minority. Claremont also promoted strong female characters such as [[Wikipedia:Rogue (comics)|Rogue]], [[Wikipedia:Storm (Marvel Comics)|Storm]] and [[Wikipedia:Moira MacTaggert|Moira MacTaggert]]. Claremont's work on ''X-Men'', which was longer than that of any other writer, defined many of the ''X-Men'' characters years after his departure from Marvel Comics. Claremont has also written several novels, including the novelization of the second X-Men film, ''[[Wikipedia:X2 (film)|X2]]''. He has a cameo in the film [[Wikipedia:X-Men 3|''X-Men 3'']] during the [[Wikipedia:Jean Grey|Jean Grey]] prologue, for which he is credited as "Lawnmower man."

===Marvel Comics===
As an entry into comic writing, Claremont was given the fledgling title [[Wikipedia:Iron Fist (comics)|''Iron Fist'']] in 1974, which teamed him with [[Wikipedia:John Byrne|John Byrne]]. [[Wikipedia:Len Wein|Len Wein]] then gave him the writing duties for the relaunched X-Men. Claremont found narrative excuses to sideline [[Wikipedia:Professor X|Professor X]], as one of the problems with the original X-Men series was that the Professor often appeared at the end of the story to provide a deus ex machina solution to whatever problem the X-Men faced.

During his years as ''X-Men'' writer, Claremont wrote or co-wrote many classic stories such as the "[[Wikipedia:Dark Phoenix Saga|Dark Phoenix Saga]]" and "[[Wikipedia:Days of Future Past|Days of Future Past]]." In addition, he helped launch best-selling spin-offs such as [[Wikipedia:X-Men (vol. 2)|''X-Men'']], [[Wikipedia:New Mutants|''New Mutants'']], [[Wikipedia:Excalibur (comics)|''Excalibur'']], and ''Wolverine''. In 1986, Marvel launched an ''X-Men'' spinoff, [[Wikipedia:X-Factor (comics)|''X-Factor'']], altering the Phoenix/Jean Grey continuity Claremont had established. In 1991 a second X-Men title simply called [[Wikipedia:X-Men (vol. 2)|''X-Men'']] (often referred as "adjectiveless" X-Men) was launched having Claremont as writer though he only wrote three issues since he left Marvel following a series of editorial differences with the X-Men editor at the time [[Wikipedia:Bob Harras|Bob Harras]].

In [[1998]], he returned to Marvel as editorial director and the regular writer of [[Wikipedia:Fantastic Four|''Fantastic Four'']]. He also wrote a ''Wolverine'' story arc. In 2000, as part of the company's "[[Wikipedia:Revolution (comics)|Revolution]]" event, he wrote ''Uncanny X-Men'' and ''X-Men'' until he moved to [[Wikipedia:X-Treme X-Men|''X-Treme X-Men'']].

In [[2006]], cardiac illness forced Claremont to postpone the beginning of a planned run on [[Wikipedia:Exiles (Marvel Comics)|''Exiles'']] as well as the launch of ''GeNext''. Also cut short was his run on ''Uncanny X-Men'', which was completed by [[Wikipedia:Tony Bedard|Tony Bedard]] over Claremont's plots. Likewise, writer Frank Tieri took over on [[Wikipedia:Excalibur (comics)|''New Excalibur'']] in Claremont's stead for a time.

In [[2007]], Claremont returned to ''New Excalibur'', writing a story arc where the character Nocturne has a stroke. He also completed his first arc on ''Exiles'', introducing Psylocke to the team.

===Career outside Marvel Comics===
Claremont has written many stories for other publishers including the [[Wikipedia:Star Trek|''Star Trek'']] ''Debt of Honor'' graphic novel, his creator-owned [[Wikipedia:Sovereign Seven|''Sovereign Seven'']] for [[Wikipedia:DC Comics|DC Comics]] and [[Wikipedia:Aliens/Predator: Deadliest of the Species|''Aliens vs Predator'']] for [[Dark Horse Comics]]. He also wrote a few issues of the series [[Wikipedia:WildC.A.T.s|WildC.A.T.s]] at [[Wikipedia:Image Comics|Image Comics]], which introduced his creator-owned character, Huntsman.

Outside of comics, Claremont co-wrote the [[Willow (film)#Star Wars connections|''The Shadow War Chronicles'']] trilogy, [[Willow (film)#Star Wars connections|''Shadow Moon'']] (1995), [[Willow (film)#Star Wars connections|''Shadow Dawn'']] (1996) and [[Willow (film)#Star Wars connections|''Shadow Star'']] (1999), with [[George Lucas]]. This trilogy continues the story of Elora Danan from the movie [[Willow (film)|''Willow'']]. He wrote a science fiction trilogy about female USAF pilot/astronaut Nicole Shea, consisting of ''First Flight'' (1987), ''Grounded!'' (1991), and ''Sundowner'' (1994). Claremont was also a contributor to the [[Wikipedia:Wild Cards|''Wild Cards'']] anthology series.
